does: This paper introduces SAGE, a post-hoc sanitization method that improves retention performance in LLM unlearning without rerunning the original unlearning pipeline. The method works by analyzing retention activation bias—the difference between activations on retained versus forgotten data—to identify which components of an unlearning update vector damage model retention. SAGE collects real module inputs from a small retain proxy dataset, extracts their dominant activation geometry using spectral analysis, and solves a source-anchored optimization problem in closed form to suppress update components aligned with high-energy retained directions while preserving the forgetting capability of the original unlearning method.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research addressing the fundamental trade-off in LLM unlearning between removing undesirable knowledge and maintaining general capabilities. It extends prior work on understanding unlearning mechanisms and activation patterns in neural networks by introducing the concept of retention activation bias as a quantifiable measure of retention damage. The paper connects to research on post-hoc model editing and correction techniques, as well as spectral methods for understanding neural network behavior. SAGE responds to limitations in existing unlearning approaches—including gradient-based, data-replay, and in-context forgetting methods—that all suffer from retention degradation, offering a method-agnostic solution that complements rather than replaces these approaches.
enables: This work opens several future directions for improving LLM unlearning at scale. The post-hoc sanitization framework could be extended to handle multiple unlearning objectives simultaneously or adapted to different model architectures and scales. The insights about activation geometry and retention bias might inform the design of unlearning methods that are inherently retention-aware from the start, rather than requiring post-hoc correction. SAGE's closed-form solution could enable efficient deployment of unlearning in practical settings where retraining is prohibitively expensive. Additionally, the approach could be generalized beyond unlearning to other model editing tasks, and the activation geometry analysis might provide tools for understanding and mitigating other forms of model drift or capability degradation.
